meta.topic: Can, could, be able to and be allowed to

step_1_context.text: We sometimes use be able to instead of can and could to talk about ability. We avoid be able to –

step_2_explanation.simple_rule: We sometimes use be able to instead of can and could to talk about ability.
step_2_explanation.formula: If we talk about a single achievement, rather than a general ability in the past, we usually use be able to rather than could.
step_2_explanation.common_mistake: To talk about the theoretical possibility of something happening we use could, not can.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 15.1.1 Valuables can / are able to be left in the hotel safe.
options: can (before passives, use can not be able to)
step_3_interactive_exercises.correct_answer: can (before passives, use can not be able to)
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Avoid 'be able to' before passives → use 'can'.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 15.1.2 We could / were able to finish the hockey match before it started snowing too heavily.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Single achievement (they finished the specific match) → were able to.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 15.1.3 The rebels could / were able to draw on the support of over 20,000 soldiers.
options: Both could and were able to are possible (general situation)
step_3_interactive_exercises.correct_answer: Both could and were able to are possible (general situation)
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: General ability/situation, not single achievement → both are possible.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 15.1.4 Could you / Were you able to understand Professor Larsen's lecture? I found it really difficult.
options: Both could you and were you able to are possible
step_3_interactive_exercises.correct_answer: Both could you and were you able to are possible
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Verb of thinking/understanding — both forms are possible.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 15.1.5 B: Sorry, I can't / I'm not able to play chess.
options: I can't (more natural — know how to)
step_3_interactive_exercises.correct_answer: I can't (more natural — know how to)
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: When meaning is 'know how to', use can/can't, not be able to.
